explanations: Masculine nouns ending in -u are declined according to the pattern of 'garu' (teacher). The nominative, vocative, and accusative singular all take the base form 'garu', while the accusative singular adds -ṃ. The instrumental and ablative singular take -nā. The dative and genitive singular have two forms: -no and -ssa. The locative singular takes -mhi or -smiṃ. In the plural, the nominative, vocative, and accusative take -ū or -avo. The instrumental and ablative plural take -ūhi (or -ūbhi). The dative and genitive plural take -ūnaṃ. The locative plural takes -ūsu.

Masculine nouns ending in -ū are declined similarly to the -u declension. Using 'vidū' (wise man) as a model, the nominative, vocative, and accusative singular all take the base form 'vidū', while the accusative singular adds -ṃ (viduṃ). The plural nominative, vocative, and accusative take -ū or -uno. The remaining cases follow the same pattern as the garu declension.

tables.title: Declension of garu (teacher) - masculine noun ending in -u
Declension of vidū (wise man) - masculine noun ending in -ū

pali_to_english: Bhikkhavo Tathāgatassa sāvakā honti.
Bandhavo ammaṃ passituṃ nagaramhā gāmaṃ āgamiṃsu.
Coro āraññe taravo chindituṃ pharasuṃ ādāya gacchi / agami.
Sīhā ca dīpayo ca aṃaviyaṃ vasante pasavo māretvā khādanti.
Sappurisā viññuno bhavanti.
Bhūpati mantīhi saddhiṃ sindhuṃ taritvā sattavo paharitvā jinituṃ ussahi.
Ammā kaṃacchunā dārikaṃ odanaṃ bhojāpesi.
Hatthino ca kaṇeruyo ca ucchavo ākaḍḍhitvā khādiṃsu.
Bhūpatissa mantino sattūnaṃ ketavo āhariṃsu.
Setumhi nisinno bandhu taruno sākhaṃ hatthena ākaḍḍhi.
Uyyāne ropitesu veḷūsu pakkhino nisīditvā gāyanti.
Sace pabhuno atthaññū honti manussā sukhino gāme viharituṃ sakkonti.
Sabbaññū Tathāgato dhammena manusse anusāsati.
Mattaññū sappurisā dīghajīvino ca sukhino ca bhaveyyuṃ.
Viññūhi anusāsitā mayaṃ kumārā sappurisā bhavituṃ ussahimha.
Mayaṃ ravino ālokena ākāse uḍḍente pakkhino passituṃ sakkoma.
Tumhe pabhuno hutvā dhammena jīvituṃ vāyameyyātha.
Ahaṃ dhammaṃ desentaṃ bhikkhuṃ jānāmi.
Ahayo ākhavo khādantā aṃaviyā vammikesu vasanti.
Vanitāya sassu bhaginiyā ucchavo ca padumāni ca adadi / adāsi.
english_to_pali: Crossing the bridge the enemy has entered the island.
You shall not cut bamboos with axes, you may with saws.
King's ministers tied banners on the bridge and on trees.
The beasts fed the young ones with rats.
The monk was a relation of the king who rules the island.
The trees cut by the enemy fell into the sea.
With the fist the mother hit the dog which was trying to bite the girl.
Kings protect recluses, brahmins, men and beasts living in the island.
Mother's sister killed a rat with a bamboo.
The teacher sent sugar-cane to the tuskers' young ones.
Seeing a monkey trying to enter the house the husband closed the door.
